description: Fires when the HTML and DOM are fully loaded (DOM ready), before images and other subresources have necessarily finished. One of three page-loading triggers: (1) page view – when the browser begins to load the page, (2) DOM ready – when the DOM is ready, (3) page load – when all page elements have loaded. Use DOM ready when you need to run tags as soon as the DOM is available, e.g. for scripts that depend on the document tree. Same event conditions as the Page view trigger (Page URL, Page path, Referrer, Campaign, etc.). See Piwik PRO help: Page view trigger.

relationship_to_page_view.note: Three loading options exist: page_view (earliest), dom_ready, page_load (latest). Use dom_ready when the tag should run after the DOM is ready but not necessarily after all assets (images, etc.) have loaded.
relationship_to_page_view.same_conditions: Event condition types and multiplicity options are the same as for the Page view trigger.

loading_conditions_reference.loading_option: Finished loading HTML and the DOM is ready (DOM ready)
alternatives: page_view – Fires when the browser begins to load a page
dom_ready – Fires when HTML and DOM are fully loaded
page_load – Fires when all page elements have finished loading

condition_types.description: Fires when a visitor visits a page with the selected page URL
Fires when a visitor visits a page with the selected page path
Fires when a visitor visits a page on the selected domain
Fires when a visitor was directed by the selected internal referrer
Fires when a visitor was directed by the selected external referrer
Fires when a visitor was directed by the selected campaign (utm_campaign)
Fires when traffic source matches (campaign, organic search, social, referral, direct)
Fires when the visitor is a returning visitor
Any variable can be used to define trigger conditions

multiplicity: Many times during a session, but skips the first event

when_to_use: Run tags as soon as the DOM is available, without waiting for images or iframes
Scripts that need to query or modify the document tree early in the load cycle
When page_view is too early and page_load is too late for your use case
common_use_cases: Analytics or tracking that only needs the DOM
Injecting or modifying content before full page load
Faster firing than page_load when you do not need all assets
best_practices: Use dom_ready when your tag does not depend on images or other subresources
Same URL/referrer condition patterns as page view; add conditions to limit when the trigger fires

create_trigger.description: Create a DOM ready trigger. Fires when the DOM is ready; same condition structure as page view.

trigger_type.description: Must be 'dom_ready' for DOM ready triggers

conditions.description: Array of condition objects; same as page view (Page URL, Page path, Referrer, etc.). Optional.

update_trigger.description: Update an existing DOM ready trigger – only editable fields will be processed

conditions.description: Array of condition objects. Same as page view: Page URL, Page path, Page hostname, Referrer, External referrer, Campaign, Traffic source, Returning visitor, or variable-based. Optional.
conditions.recommendation: Include at least one URL-based condition to control when the trigger fires.

dom_ready_all_pages_example.description: Fire on DOM ready for all pages

dom_ready_product_path_example.description: Fire on DOM ready only when Page path contains /product/

common_mistakes: Using dom_ready when the tag needs images or iframes (use page_load)
Using dom_ready when the tag can run at first paint (page_view may be enough)
